questions.question: Define statistical inference. Explain the difference between a population and a sample with suitable examples.
keyPoints: Definition of statistical inference as drawing conclusions about population from sample
Population: entire group of interest, size N, described by parameters
Sample: subset of population, size n, described by statistics
Examples: all voters vs surveyed voters, all manufactured items vs tested batch
Why sampling is necessary: cost, time, destructive testing
Diagram showing population-sample-inference relationship

questions.question: Distinguish between a parameter and a statistic. Give examples of each and explain why statistics are used to estimate parameters.
keyPoints: Parameter: numerical characteristic of population (mu, sigma, P), usually unknown, fixed
Statistic: numerical characteristic of sample (x-bar, s, p-hat), computed from data, varies across samples
Examples: population mean vs sample mean, population proportion vs sample proportion
Statistics used because population is often inaccessible
Concept of unbiased estimator: E(x-bar) = mu

questions.question: Explain the two main types of statistical inference: estimation and hypothesis testing. Describe each with examples.
keyPoints: Estimation: using sample data to estimate unknown population parameters
Point estimation: single best estimate (e.g., x-bar = 170 cm)
Interval estimation: range with confidence level (e.g., 95% CI: 168 to 172 cm)
Hypothesis testing: procedure to accept or reject claims about parameters
Null hypothesis H0 and alternative hypothesis H1
Test statistic, significance level (alpha), and decision rule
Real-world examples for each type

questions.question: What is sampling? Explain different types of sampling methods and discuss the concept of bias in sampling.
keyPoints: Sampling: process of selecting a subset from a population
Simple random sampling: equal probability for each member
Stratified sampling: dividing population into strata
Systematic sampling: selecting every k-th element
Cluster sampling: selecting entire clusters randomly
Bias: systematic error making sample unrepresentative
Types of bias: selection, response, non-response, measurement

questions.question: Write a note on the importance of statistical inference. How is it applied in the field of computer science?
keyPoints: Enables decision-making under uncertainty using limited data
Machine learning: training on samples, generalizing to unseen data
Software testing: judging software quality from test case subsets
Performance analysis: confidence intervals for benchmarks
A/B testing in data science and tech industry
Network security: anomaly detection from sampled traffic
Foundation for advanced topics in the syllabus (sampling distributions, significance tests)
